title: New OT Device Detection Alert
app: `Cisco Cyber Vision Splunk Add-On` (Splunkbase 5748) · `Nozomi Networks Universal Add-on` (Splunkbase 6905)
spl:
index=nozomi sourcetype="nozomi:alert" type_id="ASSET-NEW"
| table _time, name, ip, mac_address, zone, risk
| sort -_time
description: Any new device appearing on a baselined OT network is a potential threat — rogue devices, unauthorized laptops, or attacker implants. OT security platforms detect new components automatically and generate alerts. Immediate alerting enables rapid investigation before a threat can spread.
value: Any new device appearing on a baselined OT network is a potential threat — rogue devices, unauthorized laptops, or attacker implants. OT security platforms detect new components automatically and generate alerts. Immediate alerting enables rapid investigation before a threat can spread.
implementation: **Cyber Vision:** Forward syslog to Splunk (CEF format via TCP/TLS); alert on `component_new` events. **Nozomi:** Enable `alert` data input; filter on `type_id="ASSET-NEW"`. Both platforms detect new devices automatically. Enrich with sensor/zone location to identify physical site. Cross-reference against approved asset list. Investigate unauthorized devices within SLA.
visualization: New device alert timeline; device location map by sensor; unauthorized device table.
knownFalsePositives: Newly seen devices from VLAN mirrors, test gear, or temporary contractor laptops on process segments.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Cisco Cyber Vision Splunk Add-On` (Splunkbase 5748) · `Nozomi Networks Universal Add-on` (Splunkbase 6905).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=cisco:cybervision:syslog` · Nozomi: `sourcetype=nozomi:alert` (type_id="ASSET-NEW").
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
**Cyber Vision:** Forward syslog to Splunk (CEF format via TCP/TLS); alert on `component_new` events. **Nozomi:** Enable `alert` data input; filter on `type_id="ASSET-NEW"`. Both platforms detect new devices automatically. Enrich with sensor/zone location to identify physical site. Cross-reference against approved asset list. Investigate unauthorized devices within SLA.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=nozomi sourcetype="nozomi:alert" type_id="ASSET-NEW"
| table _time, name, ip, mac_address, zone, risk
| sort -_time
```

Understanding this SPL

**New OT Device Detection Alert** — Any new device appearing on a baselined OT network is a potential threat — rogue devices, unauthorized laptops, or attacker implants. OT security platforms detect new components automatically and generate alerts. Immediate alerting enables rapid investigation before a threat can spread.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=cisco:cybervision:syslog` · Nozomi: `sourcetype=nozomi:alert` (type_id="ASSET-NEW"). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Cisco Cyber Vision Splunk Add-On` (Splunkbase 5748) · `Nozomi Networks Universal Add-on` (Splunkbase 6905). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: nozomi; **sourcetype**: nozomi:alert. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=nozomi, sourcetype="nozomi:alert". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Pipeline stage (see **New OT Device Detection Alert**): table _time, name, ip, mac_address, zone, risk
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Step 3 — Validate
Run the search for a time range that should include both routine and change activity, then compare the same assets, flows, and alerts in the Cisco Cyber Vision UI and in Nozomi Guardian or CMC, matching whichever source the event came from. Reconcile severities, counts, and key fields in both tools.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: New device alert timeline; device location map by sensor; unauthorized device table.
grandmaExplanation: We flag devices we have not seen before on the OT side so a rogue or mis-placed box is not just another line on a diagram.
